event: Christ enthroned in glory (Christ in Majesty / Majestas Domini)
symbols: mandorla — the almond-shaped aureole of divine glory surrounding Christ, signifying His transcendence beyond earthly space
gospel book held by Christ — the Word of God, Christ as Logos, the divine teaching entrusted to the Church
four creatures in the corners — the four evangelists (Matthew as man/angel, Mark as lion, Luke as ox, John as eagle), drawn from Ezekiel 1 and Revelation 4
throne — divine kingship and the seat of final judgment
golden background — the uncreated light of God, heaven beyond time and space
decorative border with circular motifs — the cosmos ordered and contained by divine sovereignty
description: A Romanesque-style icon on wood panel depicts Christ seated on a throne within a large mandorla decorated with circular ornamental patterns. He holds a red gospel book in his left hand and raises his right hand in blessing. His face is frontal and solemn, with large eyes and a dark beard. In the four corners of the panel, partially obscured by age and wear, appear the symbols of the four evangelists: an angel, an eagle, a lion, and an ox. The background is a warm golden-orange, and the overall palette features rich earth tones, deep reds, and gold.
theological_description: The Christ in Majesty (Majestas Domini) is one of the oldest and most theologically concentrated compositions in Christian art. Rooted in the apocalyptic visions of Ezekiel 1 and Revelation 4-5, it presents Christ not as the suffering servant of the Passion but as the Pantocrator — the all-sovereign ruler of the cosmos. The mandorla signifies the unapproachable glory of God (1 Timothy 6:16), the same radiance that overshadowed Sinai and filled the Temple. Christ holds the gospel book, identifying Himself as the Logos of John 1:1 — the Word through whom all things were made and in whom all revelation is contained. The four living creatures (tetramorph) surrounding the throne derive from Ezekiel's inaugural vision (Ezek 1:5-14) and reappear in Revelation 4:6-8, where they ceaselessly proclaim 'Holy, holy, holy.' Irenaeus of Lyon first assigned the creatures to the four evangelists, each Gospel presenting a distinct facet of Christ: Matthew the incarnate man, Mark the royal lion, Luke the sacrificial ox, John the soaring eagle of divine contemplation. The enthroned posture anticipates the Parousia — Christ's return in glory to judge the living and the dead (Matthew 25:31). This icon is a window into eschatological reality: what will be fully revealed at the end of time is already present liturgically whenever the faithful gather before the image.

scripture_refs.reason: John's vision of the throne in heaven surrounded by four living creatures — the direct source of the Majestas Domini composition
Ezekiel's vision of the four living creatures bearing the throne-chariot of God, the Old Testament prototype of this iconography
The Lamb on the throne holding the sealed scroll — Christ's sovereign authority over revelation and history
Christ as the Logos, the Word made flesh, represented by the gospel book He holds
The Son of Man coming in glory to sit on His throne and judge the nations — the eschatological fulfillment this icon anticipates
God as the 'blessed and only Sovereign, dwelling in unapproachable light' — the theological reality behind the mandorla's radiance
The Ancient of Days on the throne and the Son of Man receiving dominion — the Danielic source for Christ's enthronement
Isaiah's temple vision of the Lord 'high and lifted up,' attended by seraphim crying 'Holy, holy, holy' — a liturgical parallel to the four creatures
